skill.name: observability-monitoring
skill.description: Implement structured logging, Prometheus metrics, OpenTelemetry tracing, health checks, and alerting for Node.js services.
skill.summary: Add production observability patterns for logs, metrics, traces, alerts, and service health checks.
skill.author: ArieGoldkin

security_audit.summary: The static critical finding is dismissed: the apparent command execution and weak cryptography hits are Markdown fences, comments, alert durations, imports, and normal configuration reads. The skill is not malicious, but it includes reusable logging and tracing templates that can collect request metadata, error details, and trace attributes, so publication should include a privacy warning.

medium_findings.title: Unredacted Request Metadata Logging
medium_findings.description: The request logger records query parameters, user agent, and IP address. This is useful for observability, but adopters could accidentally log tokens, personal data, or internal identifiers without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly logs request metadata that commonly contains sensitive values. The behavior is legitimate for monitoring, but it creates a real privacy risk if copied without filtering.

medium_findings.title: Telemetry Export May Send Sensitive Trace Attributes
medium_findings.description: The OpenTelemetry template exports traces to an endpoint selected by environment configuration and records order and payment attributes. This is expected observability behavior, but it can transmit sensitive operational data to a collector.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exporter and attributes are visible in the template, but the endpoint defaults to localhost and real impact depends on deployment configuration. This warrants a warning rather than a block.

medium_findings.title: Health Check Responses Expose Operational Details
medium_findings.description: The readiness endpoint returns application version, uptime, dependency names, latency, and raw error messages. These details can help operators, but they can also reveal internal system state if exposed publicly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The response fields and raw error message paths are explicit. The finding is medium because health data is normal for internal probes but should not be exposed without access control.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown and Comments
low_findings.description: The static shell execution matches are Markdown code fences, TypeScript template string examples, template references, or a commented bad logging example. No executable Ruby backtick or shell command path was found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows documentation fences and commented example text, not runtime command execution. There is no user input reaching a shell in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits are not cryptographic code. They appear in descriptive text, Prometheus alert expressions, humanized descriptions, imports, and normal control flow comments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain monitoring text or OpenTelemetry setup, not hashing, encryption, or signature verification. No weak crypto API use was found.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Configuration Only
low_findings.description: The environment variable reads configure log level, service metadata, deployment environment, trace exporter endpoint, and application version. No code reads environment files or sends all environment variables elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each environment read is a named configuration value. There is no evidence of .env file access, credential harvesting, or bulk environment exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URL Defaults To Local Collector
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL is the OpenTelemetry trace exporter default for localhost. It is a standard local collector endpoint, not an external exfiltration destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is localhost and appears in a normal OTLP exporter configuration. Network risk remains possible only when users set an external endpoint through deployment configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Log Request Data Only After Redaction
dangerous_patterns.description: The template logs query parameters and client metadata. Users should redact secrets and avoid logging authentication tokens, session identifiers, or unnecessary personal data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data capture is direct and visible, but whether it contains sensitive data depends on the application routes and requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Protect Telemetry Export Destinations
dangerous_patterns.description: The trace exporter sends spans to an OTLP HTTP endpoint. Users should restrict exporter destinations and review span attributes before enabling production tracing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exporter is legitimate observability code with a localhost default. The risk depends on production endpoint configuration and the attributes recorded by the application.

content.value_statement: Production services need consistent logs, metrics, traces, alerts, and health probes. This skill provides practical TypeScript and Prometheus templates for adding that visibility.
actual_capabilities: Provides Winston structured logging patterns with request correlation IDs.
Defines Prometheus HTTP, database, cache, and business metric examples.
Shows OpenTelemetry setup with Node.js auto-instrumentation and manual spans.
Includes Express health, readiness, and startup probe templates.
Supplies Prometheus alerting rules for service, error rate, latency, memory, database, and cache signals.
limitations: Templates are examples and must be adapted to each application architecture.
The skill does not deploy monitoring infrastructure or dashboards automatically.
Security controls such as log redaction and endpoint authentication are not fully implemented.
Examples focus on Node.js, Express, Prometheus, Winston, and OpenTelemetry.

use_cases.title: Add baseline service observability
use_cases.description: Introduce structured request logs, RED metrics, and health probes to a new Node.js service.

use_cases.title: Prepare production alerting
use_cases.description: Create Prometheus alerts for outages, error spikes, latency, memory pressure, database pool exhaustion, and cache degradation.

use_cases.title: Improve incident diagnosis
use_cases.description: Connect logs, metrics, and traces so teams can investigate request failures across service boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Add basic request logging
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an Express service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add structured request logging to my Express service. Include correlation IDs, useful fields, and safe redaction recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Create service metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design Prometheus metrics for HTTP requests, database latency, cache hits, and key business events in my Node.js app.

prompt_templates.title: Set up distributed tracing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add OpenTelemetry tracing to my service. Include auto-instrumentation, manual spans, exporter configuration, and privacy safeguards.

prompt_templates.title: Design an incident-ready monitoring package
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my service for production observability. Propose logs, metrics, traces, alerts, health checks, dashboards, and rollout steps.

output_examples.input: Add request observability to an Express API.
output: A structured logging plan with request IDs, route metadata, response status, and latency fields.
Guidance to redact query values, tokens, session identifiers, and unnecessary personal data.
A middleware placement recommendation for consistent request and response logging.

output_examples.input: Define production alerts for a web service.
output: A prioritized alert set covering service availability, error rate, latency, memory usage, database pool pressure, and cache hit rate.
Suggested severities and evaluation windows for each alert.
Notes on reducing alert noise with clear ownership and thresholds.
A tracing design with parent spans for order processing and child spans for payment and inventory work.
Recommended span attributes that avoid storing raw payment details or customer secrets.
Exporter configuration guidance for local development and controlled production collectors.

best_practices: Define redaction rules before enabling production request logging or tracing.
Start with a small alert set tied to user impact, then tune thresholds from real traffic.
Use consistent service names, versions, environments, and correlation IDs across all telemetry.
anti_patterns: Do not log full request bodies, tokens, passwords, session IDs, or unfiltered query strings.
Do not expose health, readiness, metrics, or tracing endpoints publicly without access controls.
Do not create alerts for every metric before defining ownership, severity, and response actions.

faq.question: Does this skill install a monitoring stack?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates and implementation guidance for application observability components.

faq.question: Which runtime does the skill focus on?
faq.answer: The templates focus on Node.js and Express with TypeScript examples.

faq.question: Can it be used with Prometheus?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Prometheus metrics and alerting rule examples.

faq.question: Can it be used with OpenTelemetry?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes OpenTelemetry tracing setup, auto-instrumentation, and manual span examples.

faq.question: What security review is needed before production use?
faq.answer: Review log fields, trace attributes, telemetry endpoints, health responses, and metrics exposure for sensitive data.

faq.question: Does it replace incident response planning?
faq.answer: No. It helps create signals and alerts, but teams still need ownership, runbooks, and escalation paths.
